messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_ashrae2024.pdf
Equipment class: valve

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

Higher Δt coils require less valve authority.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "valve_authority", "title": "Valve Authority Range for HVAC Coil Applications", "summary": "Valve authority between 30 to 50% is adequate for HVAC coil applications with 2:1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios. training, and construction)
• Basis of design• HV AC system analysis and selection matrix• System narratives• Budget costs (first cost, operating cost, and energy cost)• Final recommendation(s)
A brief outline of each of the final selections should be provided
along with overall budget and/or cost per square metre. In addition,
HV AC systems deemed inappropriate should be noted as havingbeen considered but not found applicable to meet the owner’s pri-mary HVAC goal.
The report should include an HV AC system selection matrix that
identifies the one or two suggested HV AC system selections (pri -
mary and secondary, when applicable), system constraints, and
other constraints and considerations.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_3be15a31257c359a", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Minimum Valve Authority to Avoid", "summary": "Valve authority less than 30% should be avoided as it causes a flow characteristic shift, making an equal-percentage valve appear more linear.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "valve_authority_minimum", "title": "Minimum Valve Authority to Avoid", "summary": "Valve authority less than 30% should be avoided as it causes a flow characteristic shift, making an equal-percentage valve appear more linear."}, "evidence": "13.16 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment (SI)

because they lead to a flow characteristic shift that makes the equal-percentage valve appear to be more linear in nature (Figures 27 and

sure drop should be from C to D.  The pressure drop in the bypass
balancing valve in the three-way valv e circuit should be set to equal
that in the coil (load), Chapter 47 discusses valve authority in threeway modulating applications.

can then be made based on sy stem performance and economic

Performance and Ratings
The long-term performance of an extended-surface air-cooling
and dehumidifying coil depends on it s correct design to specified
conditions and material specificat ions, proper matching to other
system components, proper instal lation, and proper maintenance as
required.
In accordance with AHRI Standard 410, dry-surface (sensible
cooling) coils and dehumidifying coils (which both cool and dehu-
midify), particularly those for fiel d-assembled coil banks or factory-
assembled packaged units using different combinations of coils, areusually rated within the following parameters:
Entering air dry-bulb temperature: 18 to 38°C
Entering air wet-bulb temperature: 15 to 30°C (if air is not dehu-
midified in the application, se lect coils based on sensible heat
transfer)
Air face velocity: 1 to 4 m/sEvaporator refrigerant saturation temperature: –1 to 13°C at coil
suction outlet (refrigerant vapor superheat at co il suction out-
let is 3 K or higher)
Entering chilled-water te mperature: 1.5 to 18°C
Water velocity: 0.3 to 2.4 m/s
For ethylene glycol solution: 0.3 to 1.8 m/s, –18 to 32°C entering
dry-bulb temperature, 15 to 27°C entering wet-bulb tempera-
ture, 10 to 60% aqueous glycol concentration by mass
The air-side ratio of sensible to total heat removed by dehumidi-
fying coils varies in practice from ab out 0.6 to 1.0 (i.e., sensible heat
is from 60 to 100% of the total, depending on the application).", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_e8e040112ae1ed29", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Coil Air Face Velocity Rating Range", "summary": "Coils are rated for air face velocities between 1 and 4 m/s, with dehumidifying coils for comfort applications often selected in the 2.0 to 2.5 m/s range.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "coil_air_face_velocity", "title": "Coil Air Face Velocity Rating Range", "summary": "Coils are rated for air face velocities between 1 and 4 m/s, with dehumidifying coils for comfort applications often selected in the 2.0 to 2.5 m/s range."}, "evidence": "The final choice

because they lead to a flow characteristic shift that makes the equal-percentage valve appear to be more linear in nature (Figures 27 and

sure drop should be from C to D.  The pressure drop in the bypass
balancing valve in the three-way valv e circuit should be set to equal
that in the coil (load), Chapter 47 discusses valve authority in threeway modulating applications.
Control valves should be sized on the basis of the required valve
coefficient C
v for the required pressure drop and flow. For more
information, see the section on Control Valve Sizing under Auto-
matic Valves in Chapter 47.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_6b1ee3e8d8a5079e", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Coil Freezing Prevention Threshold", "summary": "Freezing of dehumidifying coil surfaces occurs when the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "coil_freezing_threshold", "title": "Coil Freezing Prevention Threshold", "summary": "Freezing of dehumidifying coil surfaces occurs when the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated."}, "evidence": "In ho t, humid climates, the light-load
condition has a higher proportion of moisture and a correspond-
ingly lower proportion of sensible  heat. The result is higher dew
points in the conditioned spaces during light-load  conditions un-
less a special means for controlling inside dew points (e.g., reheator dual path) is used.
Fin surface freezing at light lo ads should be avoided. Freezing
occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature fallsbelow 0°C. Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort
installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the
coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C satu rated; the exact value depends on
the design of the coil, its opera ting dew point, and the amount of
loading. With coil and condensing units to balance at low tempera-
tures at peak loads (not a custom ary design choice), freezing may
occur when load suddenly decreases.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_3490506dd0cad51d", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Airflow Resistance Comparison", "summary": "A completely dry coil removing only sensible heat offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil removing both sensible and latent heat.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "coil_airflow_resistance", "title": "Airflow Resistance Comparison", "summary": "A completely dry coil removing only sensible heat offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil removing both sensible and latent heat."}, "evidence": "Low-frequency noise is  usually genera ted by mechanical
equipment and transmitted through th e duct and outlet to the room.
The cause of a noise complaint ca n usually be pi npointed as out-
let or system sounds by removing the outlet’s core during operation.
If the noise remains essentially uncha nged, the system is the source.
If the noise is significantly reduced, the outlet is likely the source.The noise may be caused by a highly irregular velocity profile at the
entrance to the outlet. ASHRAE research project RP-1335 (Lands-
berger 2011) and Chapter 58 of the 2023 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications  have more details on th e effects of inlet condi-
tions on air outlets.
Variable-Air-Volume (V A V) Outlet.  A V A V outlet is an air out-
let that self-modulates its primary air flow rate with an integral air
damper. Select outlets based on the total range of airflow needed for
the space served. Outlet performance characteristics should be eval-
uated at both minimum and maximum flow.", "source_pages": [4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_27814598b0f514a3", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Hydrophilic Coil Pressure Drop Reduction", "summary": "Hydrophilic aluminum fin surface coatings can reduce air pressure drop across a new coil by as much as 30% compared to an untreated coil.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "hydrophilic_coil_pressure_drop", "title": "Hydrophilic Coil Pressure Drop Reduction", "summary": "Hydrophilic aluminum fin surface coatings can reduce air pressure drop across a new coil by as much as 30% compared to an untreated coil."}, "evidence": "Therefore, final selection involves
economic balancing of the initial co st of the coil against the operat-
ing costs of the coil geometry co mbinations available to adequately
meet the performa nce requirements.
The aluminum fin surfaces of new dehumidifying coils tend to in-
hibit condensate sheeting action until they have aged for a year. Hy-
drophilic aluminum fin surface coatings reduce water droplet surface
tension, producing a more evenly dispersed wetted surface action at
initial start-up. Manufacturers have tried different methods of apply-
ing such coatings, including dippi ng the coil into a tank, coating the
fin stock material, or subjecting the material to a chemical etchingprocess. Tests have s hown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure
drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil..", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_80c5a9e07d14d054", "knowledge_type": "operational_sequence", "title": "Variable-Speed Pump Control Stability", "summary": "Variable-speed circulating pumps can provide stable control of coil discharge temperature, with stability on the order of ±0.28 K.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "variable_speed_pump_stability", "title": "Variable-Speed Pump Control Stability", "summary": "Variable-speed circulating pumps can provide stable control of coil discharge temperature, with stability on the order of ±0.28 K."}, "evidence": "However, the basic differences in control are
• With the two-way valve configur ation (Figure 29A), the distribu-
tion system sees variab le flow and constant t, whereas with both
three-way configurations, the distribution system  sees constant
flow and variable t.
• Configuration B differs from C in  that the pressure required
through the three-way valve in Figure 29B is provided by the load
pump, whereas in Figure 29C it is provided by the distribution
pump(s).
Alternatives to Control Valves
Use of variable-frequency drives  has greatly increased because
advances in technology and decrea ses in cost have made these
drives an attractive alternative to using control valves for heat trans-
fer control (Figure 30).
Green (1994) tested the control st ability of variable-speed circu-
lating pumps compared to control valves. Stability of the controlleddischarge temperature of  the coil was on the order of ±0.28 K.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}]}
